tweets.text:
Over the past year, @SkyEcosystem and @ethena generated almost identical fees.

→ Sky: ~$413M
→ Ethena: ~$362M

But they differ on protocol revenue:

→ Sky: ~$240M
→ Ethena: ~$12.4M

The difference comes down to their business models.

Sky earns from borrower fees and yield on stablecoin collateral. It keeps most of it at the protocol level.

In comparison, Ethena earns from hedged collateral (funding and basis trades). Most of that goes to sUSDe holders as yield.

This makes Ethena more exposed when funding rates drop. You can see it in the 1m TVL change over the past month:

→ Sky: +0.5%
→ Ethena: -16%

Both generated hundreds of millions in fees, but Sky captured more value for the protocol.

tweets.text:
I just read about the Ostium $18M exploit this morning from @DefiantNews.

Ostium halted all trading after the exploit, but the damage is already done.

If you're a stock trader, IMO there are two great alternatives to rotate into at the moment ↓

1. @TrueCurrentX

A great venue if you're interested in taking long/short positions with size.

→ Up to 25x on majors, 10x across most of the book
→ 113 US equity perp markets
→ Zero trading fees
→ Onchain + USDC-settled

There's also an active points program, so your activity counts towards a potential future airdrop.

2. @grvt_io

The right venue if you don't want to shift between apps for trading, yield, and RWA.

→ 87 RWA perp pairs (synthetic, so you trade without owning the stock)
→ Margin keeps earning yield while it backs your trade
→ Managed RWA strategies from the same balance
→ Zero gas + onchain settlement

TrueCurrent is an earlier, more asymmetric bet, while Grvt is a more established player.

I think delta-neutral strategies across these two platforms are the best way to gain exposure to both.

tweets.text:
When you enter the third stage of your life, your joy changes. It’s no longer about being successful yourself anymore—the greatest thing you can do is help others be successful.

That is why I wrote Principles in 2017.

Over my life and career, every time I encountered a situation and thought about how to make a decision, I took the time to write down the criteria I would use. They became recipes—written principles that others could look at, challenge, and use to find clarity. Eventually, we even put many of these into algorithms so computers could make decisions with us.

Writing them down changed everything for me. Now, I hope anyone who reads them can take these recipes and decide for themselves what works for them.
#Principles #Mentorship #Leadership

tweets.text:
By 2032 or 2034, crypto crosses $100 trillion.

For context... there's about $800 trillion of assets in the world right now. Gold is $30 trillion, global M2 is $80 trillion. The entire digital asset space is $3.5 trillion.

Adoption has followed the same log regression channel for over a decade. Forward-project it and you land at $100 trillion by the early 2030s.

That's $97 trillion of new wealth in under a decade. More than every Russian billionaire, US tech billionaire, and finance and real estate billionaire on earth, combined.

I've been saying this for years. This is how you unfuck your future.

tweets.text:
After thousands of pitches, here's what matters:

1. 𝐋𝐨𝐯𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐫. Not "understand" them. LOVE them. If you don't obsess over making their life better, you won't make it.

2. 𝐁𝐞 𝐨𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐜. Pessimists don't build trillion-dollar companies. They give up when it gets hard. And it WILL get hard.

3. 𝐁𝐞 𝐢𝐦𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐞𝐞𝐫 𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞. "A spouting whale gets harpooned" is the OPPOSITE of what I want. I want founders who speak up. Who don't care what everyone else thinks.

You're going to have dark moments. Jesus Christ had moments of doubt and pain (shoutout Rolling Stones). You will too.

The question isn't whether you'll face hard times. It's whether you'll push through them.

That's what separates the founders I back from everyone else.
tweets.full_text:
After thousands of pitches, here's what matters:

1. 𝐋𝐨𝐯𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐫. Not "understand" them. LOVE them. If you don't obsess over making their life better, you won't make it.

2. 𝐁𝐞 𝐨𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐜. Pessimists don't build trillion-dollar companies. They give up when it gets hard. And it WILL get hard.

3. 𝐁𝐞 𝐢𝐦𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐞𝐞𝐫 𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞. "A spouting whale gets harpooned" is the OPPOSITE of what I want. I want founders who speak up. Who don't care what everyone else thinks.

You're going to have dark moments. Jesus Christ had moments of doubt and pain (shoutout Rolling Stones). You will too.

The question isn't whether you'll face hard times. It's whether you'll push through them.

That's what separates the founders I back from everyone else.

tweets.text:
You should advocate for Strategy to stop guaranteeing an ATM hit at $100. It's extremely bad for the product to automatically ATM it at $100, it offers the shorts a quasi zero downside trade while preventing all buyers from getting short-term upside above $100, and it incentivizes all the 'arb to par' dip buyers to frontrun the $100 level on their exit.

Also expecting the instrument to trade in a 1% range with such a volatile collateral is mathematically impossible. Think about it: $STRF regularly moves +/-10% in one month, which means it's the volatility you can expect with a stable dividend rate pref. $STRC is different in theory because the div rate instead of the price should be the adjustment variable. But the div rate changes very slowly (once at month and only by 50bps max), so over a one month timeframe, we should expect similar volatility for STRC as for STRF, meaning at least a 10% range in the more extreme months.

(The only way to suppress the volatility of STRC and make it trade in a $99-$100 range would be to massively restrict its mcap, which is clearly not the goal of Strategy.)

I think Strategy should state that they will only hit the ATM between $100 and $105 at their discretion from now on.
In the very short-term, it wouldn't solve the issue of STRC trading significantly below par, but it would be a step in the right direction and incentivize more buying.

I'm pretty certain that as a former engineering student, you will end up being convinced of the impossibility of having both a stable price (1% range) and a stable div rate (on short timeframe) on a pref backed by a very volatile collateral (BTC).

thread_tweets.text:
I also think that buying back $STRC at these levels is a very bad idea. It would essentially mean "we think that we are over-levered here", just 2 months after having actively looked for higher leverage.

If Strategy truly believes that BTC will compound at 20%-30% CAGR, then they should be very happy to have 'secured' that leverage already, and there's no reason to buy STRC back.

The trust in STRC being pegged at $100 has been broken anyway now, it won't be fixed instantly by selling BTC for STRC here, which would be running the exact opposite trade as in May (with the goal of... then selling STRC at $100 to buy BTC again?)

thread_tweets.text:
It's actually very different, because the goal is not to have it trade at $105, it's to have it trade around $100, which you won't have if you strictly cap it at $100 at the same time.
Basically people like you shorting at $105 would be "useful" because they would be making sure that the instrument trades correctly (ie it reverts back to $100), whereas in the current setup, people shorting at $100 (and people selling at $100) are making the instrument trades strictly below $100 on average. If we expect a roughly 10% range, then capping the upside at $100 means STRC will likely trade around $95 on average, while Saylor wants the average to be less than 1% away from $100

tweets.text:
I drew this chart a couple months ago that was ahead of its time (blue signal on chart) 

I believed that as with all things about markets, the one to monitor is always the markets expectations about capex - and while this is hard to monitor quantitatively, I liked to look at the rate of change of capex spend - which more or less follows the "market expectation" curve 

from 40b -> 200b = 5x, +160b; from 200b -> 700b = +500b, yet it's only a ~3x

and essentially with market cycles its all about how the market will react to news rather than the news itself that matters. In this particular case, everyone knows Capex is going to go up. But the rate of change of it going up slows down, until we reach "peak saturation point" where there is no marginal buyer that can step in because what is another couple of hundred billion? 

that was basically the point I thought the market would top. I thought it would come in EOY, but as with these things, as price moves exponentially upwards, the time it would play out moves closer and closer.

and essentially with market cycles its all about how the market will react to news rather than the news itself that matters. In this particular case, everyone knows Capex is going to go up. But the rate of change of it going up slows down, until we reach "peak saturation point" where there is no marginal buyer that can step in because what is another couple of hundred billion?
